[World Weather Attribution](https://www.worldweatherattribution.org/about/)
---------------------------------------------------------------------------

World Weather Attribution uses weather observations and climate models to understand how climate change influences the intensity and likelihood of extreme weather events. The studies also assess the role of human vulnerability and exposure in the impacts to highlight the actions needed to prepare for changing weather extremes.

Most studies are performed rapidly, in the aftermath of extreme weather events – or even while they’re still happening – to answer the increasingly common question: ‘what was the role of climate change in this event?’

Formed in 2014, WWA has performed more than 90 attribution studies on heatwaves, extreme rainfall, drought, floods, wildfires and cold spells around the world.

To contact World Weather Attribution, email [wwamedia@imperial.ac.uk](mailto:wwamedia@imperial.ac.uk)

[![Image 8](https://www.worldweatherattribution.org/wp-content/uploads/alexey-ruban-R-iI2W-BC3A-unsplash-1-scaled.jpg)](https://www.worldweatherattribution.org/analysis/cold-spells/)

[Cold spells](https://www.worldweatherattribution.org/analysis/cold-spells/)
----------------------------------------------------------------------------

Unusual cold spells can occur even in a warming world, and cause disruption to transport, energy & food supplies.

[![Image 9](https://www.worldweatherattribution.org/wp-content/uploads/V-P-IQ-E-02383-min-1-scaled.jpg)](https://www.worldweatherattribution.org/analysis/drought/)

[Drought](https://www.worldweatherattribution.org/analysis/drought/)
--------------------------------------------------------------------

Drought affects people in many ways, from reduced water & food supplies to increasing the risk of wildfires.

[![Image 10](https://www.worldweatherattribution.org/wp-content/uploads/dibakar-roy-P7Z3HwNWPeQ-unsplash-5-1-scaled.jpg)](https://www.worldweatherattribution.org/analysis/rainfall/)

[Extreme rainfall](https://www.worldweatherattribution.org/analysis/rainfall/)
------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Rainfall events from a major storm or hurricane, or intense localised downpours can lead to flooding in any type of location.

[![Image 11](https://www.worldweatherattribution.org/wp-content/uploads/newseventsimage_1659436460607_mainnews2012_x4-1-scaled.jpg)](https://www.worldweatherattribution.org/analysis/heatwave/)

[Heatwave](https://www.worldweatherattribution.org/analysis/heatwave/)
----------------------------------------------------------------------

Heatwaves can be particularly dangerous to humans, and occur all over the world with increasing intensity.

[![Image 12](https://www.worldweatherattribution.org/wp-content/uploads/raychel-sanner-cswvKS59E90-unsplash-1-1-scaled.jpg)](https://www.worldweatherattribution.org/analysis/storm/)

[Storms](https://www.worldweatherattribution.org/analysis/storm/)
-----------------------------------------------------------------

Storms usually include heavy rain, snow or hail, strong winds and thunder & lightning.

[![Image 13](https://www.worldweatherattribution.org/wp-content/uploads/23-06-23-Feu344-Lebel_Sur_Quevillion-Frederic_Chouinard-OS-003-min-scaled-e1692713614151.jpg)](https://www.worldweatherattribution.org/analysis/wildfire/)

[Wildfire](https://www.worldweatherattribution.org/analysis/wildfire/)
----------------------------------------------------------------------

Warmer temperatures are increasing the likelihood of wildfires both starting and spreading in many regions of the world.

![Image 14: World Weather Attribution Logo](https://www.worldweatherattribution.org/wp-content/themes/WorldWeatherAttribution/img/footer-logo.png)

--- SOURCE 5: Event papers - World Weather Attribution ---
URL: https://www.worldweatherattribution.org/event-papers/

SUMMARY:
Our studies are published just days or weeks after an extreme weather event. We do this to help inform conversations about climate change and preparations

enriched_points.expanded_content: World Weather Attribution conducts rapid assessments of extreme weather events, providing timely insights on the role of climate change in events like floods and heatwaves.
The project reveals how human-induced climate change exacerbates extreme weather, highlighting recent studies linking severe rainfall and heatwaves to increasing global temperatures.

By publishing findings shortly after extreme events, World Weather Attribution enhances public understanding of climate impacts, encouraging informed discussions and proactive climate resilience measures.
